name: Prefixes (Milli, Kilo, Mega...)
description: Standard prefixes that make units bigger or smaller (like kilo-, milli-, mega-)

content.overview: Instead of writing 0.001 meters, we say 1 millimeter. Instead of 1000 grams, we say 1 kilogram. Prefixes are shortcuts! {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
Physics deals with really big and really small things. Atoms are tiny. Galaxy distances are huge. Writing out all the zeros gets annoying. So scientists use prefixes. {{viz:diagram-02}}

A prefix is just a shorthand for a power of 10:

**Going BIG (multiply by powers of 10):**
- kilo (k) = × 1,000 = 10³
- mega (M) = × 1,000,000 = 10⁶
- giga (G) = × 1,000,000,000 = 10⁹
- tera (T) = × 1,000,000,000,000 = 10¹²

**Going SMALL (divide, or × negative powers of 10):**
- centi (c) = ÷ 100 = 10⁻²
- milli (m) = ÷ 1,000 = 10⁻³
- micro (μ) = ÷ 1,000,000 = 10⁻⁶
- nano (n) = ÷ 1,000,000,000 = 10⁻⁹
- pico (p) = ÷ 1,000,000,000,000 = 10⁻¹²

**Real Examples:** {{viz:chart-01}}
- 1 kilometer (km) = 1,000 meters (not 1 million meters!)
- 1 millimeter (mm) = 0.001 meters
- 1 microgram (μg) = 0.000001 grams
- 1 nanosecond (ns) = 0.000000001 seconds

The prefix always comes before the unit. {{viz:anim-01}}

**Why prefixes matter:**
- Shorter to write: 5 km instead of 5,000 m
- Easier to read: 2 mm instead of 0.002 m
- Matches the scale: A millimeter LOOKS tiny (mm), a kilometer SOUNDS big (km)
- Scientists use the right prefix for the measurement: measuring atoms? Use nanometers. Measuring cities? Use kilometers.
keyPoints: mega-, giga-, tera- for really big numbers
content.whyItMatters: You'll see prefixes everywhere in physics. Understanding them makes reading scientific measurements easy instead of confusing.
